retrieved_docs.title: Thoracentesis -- Indications -- Pneumonia and parapneumonic effusion
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculosis: TB pleuritis can lead to exudative effusions; extracted fluid can be sent for acid-fast staining and culture.

retrieved_docs.title: Pleurisy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of pleural effusion, thoracentesis may be necessary to analyze pleural fluid and identify the underlying cause. Ultrasound of the chest can guide the procedure by locating the fluid and possibly detecting pleural masses, while computed tomography (CT) may be required for further clarification of the cause. Fluid obtained during thoracentesis is tested for pH, glucose, cell count, lactate dehydrogenase, and bacterial Gram stain and cultures. Clinicians use Light criteria to classify the effusion as either a transudate or exudate. Specific tests, such as an acid-fast bacillus smear, adenosine deaminase level, and tuberculosis culture, are required for patients with suspected tuberculosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Follow-on testing for detection of drug resistance after TB confirmation
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculous pleural effusions are challenging to diagnose. Adenosine deaminase (ADA) detection in pleural fluid supports the diagnosis with a pooled sensitivity and specificity of approximately 92% and 90%, respectively. ADA detection is simple and inexpensive to perform and provides a rapid result. Detection of ADA in cerebral spinal fluid for the diagnosis of TB meningitis has a pooled sensitivity and specificity of approximately 85% and 90%. Results from a few studies have suggested the value of ADA levels of peritoneal and pericardial fluid for suspected TB at these sites. [52] [53] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Empyema -- Evaluation -- Thoracentesis
retrieved_docs.content: In certain cases, additional testing may be necessary. For example, pleural fluid should be analyzed in patients with suspected tuberculosis using acid-fast bacilli stain, culture, and polymerase chain reaction testing, especially in high-risk populations or regions with a high tuberculosis prevalence. This comprehensive diagnostic approach ensures accurate identification of the underlying cause and informs the optimal therapeutic strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: Pleural Friction Rub -- Evaluation -- Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with pleural effusion and risk factors for tuberculous infection should undergo tuberculosis evaluation beginning with a chest radiograph and 3 sputum specimens obtained 8 hours apart for acid-fast bacillus (AFB), mycobacterial culture, and nucleic acid amplification testing. One sample should be an early morning sample.

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracentesis -- Indications -- Diagnostic Indications for Thoracentesis
retrieved_docs.content: When thoracentesis is indicated for diagnosis, the Light criteria are instrumental in distinguishing between exudates and transudates. The analysis of pleural aspirate should routinely include a Gram stain, differential cell count, culture, cytology, protein levels, l-lactate dehydrogenase, and pH. Additional tests, such as tuberculosis (TB) screening, should be considered based on individual patient risk factors and regional prevalence. Typically, diagnostic thoracentesis extracts a small volume (20-30 mL) of accumulated fluid. Diagnostic indications for thoracentesis include:

retrieved_docs.title: Pleural Friction Rub -- Evaluation -- Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with poor immune status, including people with human immunodeficiency virus and CD4 counts below 100 cells/mm³, should also undergo blood and urine mycobacterial cultures. Clinicians obtain a pleural fluid specimen for AFB smears, mycobacterial cultures, and measurement of adenosine deaminase (ADA), an enzyme produced from lymphocytes and involved in purine metabolism. [16] AFB smears and mycobacterial cultures of pleural fluid are only positive in approximately 50% of cases, and the results can take several weeks. Pleural fluid ADA levels can establish a presumptive diagnosis in patients with lymphocyte-predominant effusions and who live in areas with a high tuberculosis incidence. The pleural fluid in patients with tuberculosis almost always has a total protein content above 4 g/dL. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Initial diagnostic testing of TB with drug-resistance testing
retrieved_docs.content: The sensitivity of Xpert and Xpert Ultra assays in detecting pulmonary TB in children is lower than in adults, likely due to the difficulty of obtaining adequate sputum samples in children. Both Xpert and Xpert Ultra assays are valuable in detecting tuberculosis from samples obtained at extrapulmonary sites, most notably cerebrospinal fluid and lymph node aspirates. The WHO 2021 update document details the sensitivity and specificity of the test for various extrapulmonary specimens. The WHO recommends using Xpert or Xpert Ultra assays as initial tests in adults and children, including PLHIV, with signs and symptoms of pulmonary TB. The tests can analyze a variety of specimens, including gastric or nasopharyngeal aspirates and stool specimens in children. The WHO also recommends their use as an initial diagnostic test in adults and children suspected of having extrapulmonary TB. For PLHIV suspected of having disseminated TB, the WHO recommends using the Xpert and Xpert Ultra assays on blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Mycobacterium-Specific Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The value of IGRA in peritoneal fluid has not been established. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Immunology-Based Methods
retrieved_docs.content: Serological tests are not advocated for TB detection because of their low sensitivity and specificity. However, adenosine deaminase (ADA) and interferon-γ (IFN-γ) may be used as adjuncts for detecting the presence of MTB in pleural, pericardial, and ascitic fluids. [38] [39] [40] [41] ADA may also be used in diagnosing TBM. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Extrapulmonary TB lesions may be assessed by ultrasonography, CECT, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which can determine the extent of organ involvement. [31] Positron-emission tomographic CT has been recently used as an investigating tool for evaluating patients with suspected TB. [32] [33] [34] Invasive diagnostic procedures are indicated for patients with suspected extrapulmonary TB. The following have a good diagnostic yield in miliary TB: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) Pleural fluid Ascitic fluid Gastric aspirate Urine Pus from a cold abscess Biopsy and culture of bone marrow and liver tissue

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Adjunctive Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid in the pelvic cavity (free or loculated) is a characteristic finding.

retrieved_docs.title: Ghon Complex -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of primary pulmonary tuberculosis heavily relies on the culture of M. tuberculosis from the sputum, [15] nasopharyngeal aspirate, [16] gastric aspirate, [17] pleural fluid, [18] or pleural biopsy. [18] Nucleic acid amplification tests (such as the Xpert MTB/RIF assay), serological tests, and chest radiography support the diagnosis of pulmonary TB. [19] The Ghon complex may be absent in patients with primary pulmonary TB. [20] Researchers estimate that in about 15% of cases of primary TB, a Ghon focus may develop, while 9% of cases of primary TB may develop tuberculomas (Figure 4), and lymphadenopathy presents in children and adults in only 96% and 43% of cases, respectively. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure -- Sputum Specimen
retrieved_docs.content: For certain diseases like mycobacterium tuberculosis ( M. tuberculosis ), a series of 3 early morning specimens, 8-10 mL in quantity, are collected and stored in a refrigerator. In uncooperative patients, M. tuberculosis can also be recovered from gastric contents by stomach aspiration.

retrieved_docs.title: Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Chest radiography may also be helpful for cardiac presentations or for those patients who need evaluation for pulmonary infections. If chest radiography does not reveal an obvious pulmonary process and there is still concern for lung pathology, computed tomography of the chest may be a consideration for further evaluation. Arterial blood gas analysis will aid the provider in determining if steroids are required for a patient with Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP).  Blood cultures should be performed before starting antibiotics in the AIDS patient.  Sputum cultures from induction or bronchoalveolar lavage, or serum and urine bacterial antigen testing, may identify the causative organism of a pulmonary infection.  Testing for tuberculosis (TB) should be performed if signs and symptoms are suggestive of the disease.  In-depth testing with advanced diagnostic imaging, such as CT, may be necessary if extrapulmonary or disseminated TB is a possibility, specifically in the more immunocompromised group of patients with AIDS, as presenting signs may be unclear. Patients suspected of having tuberculosis should be placed in respiratory isolation even before confirmatory testing is returned.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Pleural Effusion -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic thoracentesis
retrieved_docs.content: The standard panel of tests that must be performed on a pleural fluid sample includes pleural fluid protein, glucose, pH, lactate dehydrogenase, cytology, and microbiology. [111] About 40 to 60 cc of pleural fluid is considered optimum for diagnosing MPE. [112] While the yield of pleural fluid analysis approaches 6% to 32% for diagnosing mesothelioma, the test has been shown to have a comparatively higher sensitivity in diagnosing adenocarcinoma (80%). [113] [114] While primarily exudative, transudative MPE may be seen in 5% to 10% of cases. [115] Repeat procedures may increase the yield by a third; however, more than 2 repeat procedures are less productive. [116]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: (اضطراب جنبي), which also includes pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. An infectious link is noted between these pleural disorders and دبيلة (tuberculosis). Closed chest drainage, thoracentesis, and related interventions are linked to the thorax subdivision and to the mediastinum through imaging findings. Overall, the knowledge base integrates anatomical locations (mediastinum, pleural cavity), disease categories (other lower respiratory disease, mediastinal disorder, pleural disorder), imaging findings (mediastinal masses, effusion, fistulas), and therapeutic procedures (chest tube placement, pleuroperitoneal shunt, underwater‑seal drain) to support accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2132
retrieved_docs.content: The finding described as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion” is classified as an “اضطراب منصفي” (segmental disorder). This indicates that the presence of fluid accumulation in the mediastinum identified on chest CT represents a localized, segmental pathological process. Recognizing mediastinal effusion on imaging as a segmental disorder helps clinicians diagnose and manage thoracic conditions that are confined to specific anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 865
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure described as incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage includes the specific technique رشف التجويف الجنبي, indicating that pleural cavity aspiration is a component of the broader chest drainage and thoracentesis interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 80
retrieved_docs.content: and air‑leak complications of the pleural space. Together, the imaging modalities, anatomical relationships, and procedural interventions provide an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal pathology and its pleural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal paracentesis is a medical procedure that is directly associated with الاسْتِسْقَاء (ascites). It is performed to obtain peritoneal fluid for diagnostic evaluation or to therapeutically drain excess fluid in patients who have ascites, thereby assisting clinicians in determining the underlying cause of the fluid accumulation and providing symptom relief.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2137
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest has a relationship that it is a subdivision of thorax, indicating that this closed‑system chest tube technique is classified within thoracic procedures. As a subdivision of thorax, closed drainage of chest is employed to evacuate air, fluid, or blood from the pleural space in conditions such as pneumothorax, hemothorax, or pleural effusion, and its categorization guides clinicians in selecting appropriate thoracic interventions and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: group. The most precise form, chest‑tube placement (تركيب أنبوب الصدر), is a narrow subset of this procedural hierarchy. Overall, the framework delineates how imaging, diagnostic aspiration, and a spectrum of chest‑tube and shunt procedures are anatomically anchored to the chest and pleural spaces and interrelated with pleural disorders such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, pulmonary collapse, and pleural neoplasms, thereby guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: behavior of the pleura, hemothorax, empyema and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right, left, bilateral). Diagnostic evaluation relies chiefly on computed tomography of the chest (CT scan chest), a thoracic imaging procedure that encompasses non‑contrast diagnostic scans, contrast‑enhanced axial tomography, and specialized sub‑procedures for detecting mediastinal findings such as superior‑mediastinal mass, posterior‑mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion (اضطراب منصفي) and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Chest CT is hierarchically placed within imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract and is associated with related modalities such as pulmonary angiographic imaging (صورة وعائية رئوية). Therapeutically, interventions are organized under the parent procedure “incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage.” Narrower techniques include pleural cavity aspiration (رشف التجويف الجنبي), insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, tube

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: the parent procedure “incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage.” Narrower techniques include pleural cavity aspiration (رشف التجويف الجنبي), insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, tube thoracostomy with a water seal, chest‑tube placement (تركيب أنبوب الصدر), closed drainage of the chest, and creation of a pleuroperitoneal shunt (which also involves incision of the chest wall and pleura). These procedures target pleural pathologies (abscess, hemothorax, empyema) and mediastinal complications, providing coordinated diagnostic and therapeutic pathways for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: The subdivision of thorax, identified in Arabic as دبيلة, corresponds to the pleural membrane and is directly linked to pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. These three conditions all involve the pleura: pleurisy denotes inflammation of the pleural layers, pneumothorax refers to the presence of air within the pleural space that disrupts normal lung expansion, and pulmonary collapse (atelectasis) often results from pleural pathology that prevents the lung from remaining inflated. Recognizing that the subdivision of thorax (دبيلة) is the anatomical structure underlying these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic intervention, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 447
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.

Thorax as a subdivision also relates to the mediastinum and the same pleural conditions. Computerized axial tomography (CT) includes the child التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر (CT scan of the chest), of which ct scan chest is a parent; other chest x‑ray is a sibling parent, placing conventional radiography within the chest imaging hierarchy. Procedurally, creation of a pleuroperitoneal shunt, insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, and tube thoracostomy (with water‑seal) all have direct or indirect procedural sites in the pleurální dutina (pleural cavity). These interventions are classified under “incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage” and are linked to incision of the chest wall and pleura, which is directly related to the pleura. The pleura is a child of اضطراب جنبي (pleural disorder) and is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: دبيلة is associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, indicating that individuals with دبيلة may develop these respiratory complications. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.
